hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Increasing the dummy‑core bond lengths (CGD2_DUP_bl, SUL_DUN_bl) separates the opposite charges on the dummy beads, thereby raising the molecular dipole moment (dipole ∝ charge × separation). Simultaneously, making the Lennard‑Jones epsilon more negative (more attractive) strengthens intermolecular cohesion, which should raise liquid density, heat of vaporization, and surface tension. Adjusting the dummy charges symmetrically (±) preserves overall neutrality while controlling dipole magnitude.

proposal.reasoning: The diagnostics show severe overlap and excessive forces causing crashes. To restore a stable liquid we (1) increase the dummy‑core bond lengths toward their upper allowed values, (2) lower the dummy‑core bond force constants to the minimum allowed (100 kcal·mol⁻¹·Å⁻²) to soften forces, (3) reduce angle stiffness to the lower bound (80) to give the molecule more flexibility, (4) make the Lennard‑Jones core repulsion larger (increase rmin) and slightly weaken attraction (make ε less negative) to prevent cores from collapsing into each other, and (5) increase the magnitude of the opposite dummy charges to boost the dipole moment. Four forks are created to explore combinations of bond length, charge magnitude, LJ size, and ε, ranging from conservative to aggressive adjustments, while keeping all values inside the prescribed bounds and away from the known crash region.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Increasing the dummy‑core bond lengths reduces the likelihood that dummy beads sit inside the core bead volume, while lowering the dummy‑core bond force constants (to the allowed minimum) lessens the impulsive forces that drive atoms to extreme velocities. Softening the angle force constants gives the molecule more conformational freedom, helping it to pack realistically. Enlarging the LJ core bead size (rmin) and making the LJ epsilon slightly less attractive adds steric repulsion that counters the previously observed extreme overlap and pressure. Finally, increasing the magnitude of the opposite dummy charges (+/-0.35–0.40 e) directly raises the molecular dipole moment, moving the dipole toward the target value.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: These changes should eliminate the atom‑velocity blow‑up, lower the simulated pressure to realistic values, and produce a stable liquid phase. The larger dipole moment from stronger dummy charges, together with appropriate bond lengths, is expected to improve the predicted dipole moment without sacrificing density. The adjusted LJ parameters and softer bonds/angles should also bring density, heat‑of‑vaporization, and surface tension closer to experimental targets.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be evaluated by running four parallel simulations, each using one of the proposed parameter sets (forks). Comparison of stability metrics (pressure, kinetic energy), thermodynamic properties, and dipole moment will reveal which combination best satisfies the targets.

Assigning equal‑and‑opposite charges ensures a pure dipole without net charge. Adjusting angle stiffness and equilibrium angles controls the orientation of the dummy beads relative to the core, further fine‑tuning the dipole vector and molecular shape, which can influence surface tension.

proposal.reasoning: The diagnostics show low density (under‑packed liquid), a slightly low heat of vaporization, a surface tension that is a bit too high, and a dipole moment that is marginally high. To raise density and ΔHvap we need stronger van der Waals cohesion (more negative ε) and tighter packing (smaller LJ size). To keep surface tension from increasing further we counterbalance the stronger ε by shrinking the LJ size parameters, which also helps density. The dipole can be lowered by shortening the dummy‑core bonds and reducing the dummy charges. Four forks are generated to (1) combine moderate ε increase, modest size reduction and slight bond/charge shrinkage (balanced exploitation), (2) apply an aggressive combination of both ε and size changes plus larger bond/charge shrinkage (exploratory extreme), (3) test size reduction only (isolating packing effect), and (4) test ε increase only (isolating cohesion effect). This provides a diverse yet physically motivated sampling of the parameter space while staying away from known crash regions.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Increasing the magnitude of the Lennard‑Jones epsilon (making it more negative) strengthens intermolecular attraction, which should raise liquid density and heat of vaporization. Decreasing the LJ size parameters (rmin and rminby2) reduces excluded volume, further increasing packing density while also tending to lower surface tension. Shortening the dummy‑core bond lengths and reducing the dummy charges decrease the separation of opposite charges, thereby lowering the molecular dipole moment.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Higher density and ΔHvap will move the thermodynamic predictions toward the experimental targets; the opposing size reduction will mitigate the surface‑tension increase caused by a stronger ε, keeping surface tension close to the desired value. The reduced bond lengths and charges will bring the dipole moment down to the target without compromising packing.
hypothesis.test_method: The four parallel forks defined in the 'action' section will be simulated and evaluated for density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment at 298 K and 323 K. Improvements relative to the current best (f = 23.23) will confirm or reject the hypothesis.
